metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.medicinal_uses: Original app included medicinal notes or related use context for this entry. Detailed copyback is still pending.
records.culinary_uses: Do not eat from this broad group record. Use it to recognize scaber-stalk boletes, then identify a source-cleared species or tight complex.
records.general_notes: Representative Commons images currently use Leccinum scabrum to illustrate the broader scaber-stalk birch/aspen bolete group.
records.duplicate_note: Broad scaber-stalk duplicate/group page kept only as a legacy slug alias. Its caution content has been consolidated into Birch Bolete / Leccinum scabrum.
records.field_identification: Pored boletes with rough, dark-dotted or scabered stalks. Look for a sponge-like pore surface instead of gills, a pale stalk covered with rough dots that often darken with age, and flesh that may stain gray, blue-gray, pinkish-gray, purplish-gray, or blackish after cutting. Host tree is important: many are associated with aspen, birch, oak, or other northern hardwoods. Do not identify these by cap color alone.

field_measurements.cap: Representative L. scabrum cap about 4–10 cm wide / about 1.5–4 in wide
field_measurements.stem: Representative L. scabrum stem about 5–15 cm long and 1–3.5 cm wide / about 2–6 in long and 3/8–1 3/8 in wide
field_measurements.notes: Group record: representative L. scabrum measurements, not a definitive range for every scaber-stalk bolete.

pore_color: Whitish when young; grayish with age in representative birch bolete
cap_surface: Tan to brown cap in representative birch bolete
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall.
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Olive-brown to brown at group level
bruising_staining: blue staining at stem base in some species
taste: not recommended as an ID trait
research_notes: Group-level spore color is useful only as a supporting trait; host and staining behavior remain more important.

records.edibility_detail: A broad Leccinum/scaber-stalk label is not sufficient for food use. Host tree, scabers, pores, staining over time, freshness, and species-level support are required.

completion_gaps: Group-level record: measurements and images are representative of Leccinum scabrum, not a definitive measurement range for every scaber-stalk bolete.
Keep as needs_review until this broad group is either split into species records or explicitly retained as a caution-group page.
records.look_alike_notes: Can be confused with other boletes, especially by beginners using cap color alone. The rough scabered stalk is the main group clue. Species-level ID can be difficult in North America, and some older names from European field guides are misapplied here. Avoid old, buggy, soft, or strongly decayed specimens. Some scaber-stalk boletes have caused gastrointestinal upset, so this group stays caution-level rather than a casual beginner edible.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject soft, bug-riddled, sour-smelling, waterlogged, blackened, mushy-pored, or badly decayed specimens. Avoid old orange-capped specimens and any collection where host/tree context and scabered stalk are not clear.
records.image_needs: Needs host-context habitat, close-up of scabered stalk, young/mature pore surfaces, cut flesh staining sequence, stem-base staining, and reject/old specimen examples.
records.safety: Caution / comparison group; not an app-approved food record
records.food_value: Not recommended
records.quality_notes: Often softens, bugs easily, and may darken or blacken when cooked; quality varies by species, age, and host association.
records.eating_use_notes: No app-approved food use from this broad group record.
records.risk_reason: Some Leccinum/scaber-stalk boletes have caused serious gastrointestinal illness even when collected as edible boletes. Species-level ID is difficult in North America.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal tract and dehydration risk; nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, intestinal cramps, sweating, and weakness can occur.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Scaber-like dark dots or scales on the stalk, orange/red/brown caps, staining reactions, mixed bolete collections, and confusion among Leccinum species. Some problem cases involve red/orange-capped Leccinum-type mushrooms.
records.hard_rule: Do not use the broad Leccinum group as edible authorization. Unknown scaber-stalk boletes stay out of the basket.
records.caution_reason: Difficult species-level identification and documented gastrointestinal reactions in wild-mushroom cases.

use_links.notes: General gastrointestinal-poisoning guidance; supports conservative response framing, not species-level edibility.

records.edibility_notes: Broad identification group only; use a separate species-level record and outside expert confirmation for any food decision.
records.mushroom_card_note: Broad scaber-stalk comparison record; host tree, scabers, pores, staining, and species-level confirmation all matter.

records.edibility_detail: Some field guides describe this as edible, but North American adverse reactions and difficult scaber-stalk identification keep it out of normal food routing here.
records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as a normal food target in this guide while North American Leccinum identification and adverse-reaction concerns remain unresolved.
records.overview: Aspen-associated scaber-stalk bolete with orangish-brown cap and white stem with dark scabers.
records.notes: Added to resolve scaber-stalk look-alike references. Keep conservative because Leccinum taxonomy and reactions are messy.
records.field_identification: Confirm an aspen/poplar association, an orange-brown to reddish-brown cap, pale pores that brown rather than rapidly blue, and a white stem covered with dark scabers. Slice the mushroom and watch the flesh and stem base for slow gray or blue-green changes.
records.habitat: With aspen/poplar in northern woods and mixed forests.

pore_color: Bruising brownish rather than vivid blue
bruising: May discolor gray/brown; not blue-staining red-pored bolete
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Tannish to olive-brown
mushroom_profile.hard_rule: Do not treat unknown scaber-stalk boletes as casual edibles.
mushroom_profile.summary: Aspen-associated orange-brown scaber-stalk bolete with pale pores, darkening scabers, and pale flesh that may gray or blue near the stem base.
substrate: Forest soil near aspen or poplar
staining: Blue-green staining may occur near stem base
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through October with aspen/poplar; local collections still require careful confirmation.
research_notes: Food route retained only as advanced edible-with-caution; the record remains review-marked for source and local-voucher strength.

mushroom_profile.summary: Birch-associated brown scaber-stalk group; exact North American application of the European name Leccinum scabrum remains review-sensitive.
mushroom_profile.host_certainty: high for birch association; exact species unresolved
texture: Quickly soft or spongy with age
staining: Usually little change or slow gray-brown discoloration
Observe cut flesh for at least 30 minutes
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Olive-brown to brown
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through October with birch.
research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Treat as a birch-associated group unless a modern local source or voucher supports the exact species name.

records.edibility_detail: European birch boletes are widely eaten, but North American name application and individual reactions remain unresolved. Keep this record for identification, not meal authorization.
records.field_identification: Birch-associated bolete with a tan to brown cap, whitish pores aging grayish, and a white or pale stem covered with dark scabers. Slice and observe for slow discoloration. Pale bog forms may instead fit Leccinum holopus; pale oak forms fit Leccinum albellum.

lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern

mushroom_profile.summary: Pale northern birch-associated scaber-stalk of boggy or wet habitats, with variable slow pink or basal blue staining.
staining: Pores may bruise a little brown
Flesh may remain white or slowly turn pinkish
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Brown
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; northern North America, including documented Michigan and Wisconsin collections.
research_notes: MushroomExpert documents Michigan and Wisconsin collections and a range from roughly the 43rd parallel northward.
The oak-associated pale look-alike Leccinum albellum may require host-tree knowledge or microscopy for confident separation.
stem_feature: Whitish to dark scabers on pale stem

records.commonness: Occasional in suitable northern wet birch habitat

records.mushroom_card_note: Pale northern birch scaber-stalk of bogs and wet woods; completed as an ID-only record.
records.field_identification: Pale brown to nearly white cap in wet birch habitat; creamy-white pores that bruise a little brown; whitish stem with whitish, brown, or nearly black scabers. Flesh may remain white, slowly turn pinkish, or show a little blue near the stem base.

mushroom_profile.summary: Small long-stemmed oak associate with sticky orange cap, dull yellow pores, and fine pinkish-brown scabers.
staining: May turn a little pinkish in stem
May blue slightly at stem base
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Olive brown
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall east of the Great Plains; Upper Michigan occurrence requires confirmation.
research_notes: MushroomExpert reports eastern North American collections but does not document an Upper Michigan collection in the cited account.
Bright cherry-red KOH on the cap is a specialist supporting test, not a beginner field shortcut.
pore_color: No blue bruising; may brown slightly

records.edibility_detail: Do not eat from this app entry. Use it for recognition and comparison only.
records.mushroom_card_note: Oak-associated eastern scaber-stalk with sticky orange cap and fine reddish-brown scabers; Upper Michigan range still on HOLD.
records.field_identification: Small, slender oak-associated bolete with a sticky orange to orange-brown cap, dull yellow pores that do not blue, and a long pale-yellowish stem with fine pale-brown to reddish-brown scabers. Flesh usually does not stain, but may turn slightly pink in the stem or blue at the base.

lookalike_risk.summary: Leccinum/scaber-stalk boletes are not beginner-safe because taxonomy is difficult, host/staining matter, and GI upset is reported in the group.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not treat unknown scaber-stalks as casual edibles.

lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

mushroom_profile.summary: Michigan-described oak associate with pitted/cracked brown cap, fine longitudinal scabers, and blue-green staining.
staining: Pores slowly dark brown, sometimes through blue
Cap margin and stem base may stain blue-green
Flesh slowly gray, sometimes through faint pink
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Not recorded in the cited species account
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer with oaks; species described from Illinois and Michigan.
research_notes: Leccinellum quercophilum is a previous combination; MushroomExpert follows the broadened Leccinum treatment.
Do not invent a print color: the cited species account states that the spore print was not recorded.
cap_surface: Tightly wrinkled and pitted when young
stem_feature: Fine brown scabers in vague longitudinal rows
pore_color: Whitish to grayish brown when young
Yellowish brown to brown with age

records.edibility_detail: Do not eat from this app entry. Use it for recognition and comparison only.
records.mushroom_card_note: Michigan-described oak scaber-stalk with pitted/cracked cap and blue-green staining; ID-only, not a food target.
records.field_identification: Oak-associated bolete with a tightly wrinkled and pitted dull brown cap that cracks with age, whitish-gray pores aging yellow-brown, tiny brown scabers in faint longitudinal rows, and greenish-blue staining near the cap margin or stem base. Cut flesh slowly turns gray, sometimes through a faint pink stage.

Confirm the exact host tree, cap surface and color, pore color, scaber color/pattern, and flesh staining over at least 30 minutes before applying this name. This record is not cleared as an Upper Michigan food target.

lookalike_risk.label: High — scaber-stalk GI/taxonomy caution
lookalike_risk.summary: Leccinum/scaber-stalk boletes are not beginner-safe because taxonomy is difficult, host/staining matter, and GI upset is reported in the group.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not treat unknown scaber-stalks as casual edibles.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not treat unknown scaber-stalks as casual edibles.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended as a normal food target in this guide while the North American orange-birch complex and adverse-reaction history remain unresolved.

stem_feature: Dense dark gray-brown to black scabers
staining: Flesh may turn pinkish or gray, then dark gray to blackish
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through October with birch; exact North American species application remains review-sensitive.
mushroom_profile.summary: Orange-capped birch-associated scaber-stalk group with dark scabers and flesh that may pink, gray, or blacken.
mushroom_profile.host_certainty: high for birch association; exact species unresolved
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Yellow-brown to olive-brown
research_notes: Use the group-level label until a modern North American source or voucher supports an exact species determination.

records.edibility_detail: Often described as edible after thorough cooking, but exact North American species application and reaction reports keep it in identification/caution routing.
records.field_identification: Birch-associated orange to orange-brown scaber-stalk with pale grayish pores and a pale stem densely covered in dark scabers. Cut flesh may pass through pink or gray tones and become dark gray to blackish. Similar orange-capped North American Leccinum require host and taxonomy checks.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: the genus Leccinum
use_links.notes: North American Leccinum key and species-complex context.

use_links.label: NAMA: Mushroom Poisoning Syndromes
use_links.notes: General GI-poisoning guidance; not species-level edible proof.

records.look_alike_notes: Separate from brown birch bolete and other orange scaber-stalks by cap color, host clues, staining, pore surface, and scaber pattern. Do not rely on cap color alone.
records.overview: Orange-capped birch-associated scaber-stalk bolete with pale pores and a rough, dark-scabered stem. It is treated here as a cautious edible only after firm identification and thorough cooking.
records.edibility_notes: Retained for identification and comparison; do not treat this record as meal authorization.
records.general_notes: Source/status cleanup v4.3.170: Wikipedia-only edible-use support is not enough to clear mushroom review; keep visible and caution-framed pending stronger source support.

edible_use.notes: No app-approved food use while this record remains under review.

records.hard_rule: Do not eat from this app record. Birch host, scabers, pores, staining, and expert taxonomic confirmation are all required before any outside food decision.
records.caution_reason: Preparation risk, reported group reactions, and unresolved North American species boundaries.
records.review_blocker: European-name application and exact Upper Michigan species resolution remain uncertain; broad orange-capped Leccinum should not be treated as one species.
records.risk_reason: Orange-capped Leccinum are difficult to separate, and raw, undercooked, or misidentified specimens have a history of gastrointestinal problems.
